coredata.dc:description: Asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) have emerged as promising, high-performance energy storage devices owing to their broad operating voltage range, which significantly improves their energy and power densities. This work thus developed a hybrid ASC consisting of the copper hydroxy compound, brochantite/malachite (BM), as a positive electrode, and the corn leaf-derived activated carbon (AC) as a negative electrode, utilizing dual-hybrid capacitive mechanisms. BM is a promising positive electrode material due to its favorable faradaic reaction, whereas the corn leaf-derived AC displayed a high surface area of 898.3 m2 g−1 with micropores, enabling for a non-faradic storage mechanism at a negative electrode. The effects of different capacitance enhancers were further studied by investigating the capacitance efficiency of each electrode after adding corn leaf-derived carbon dot (CD) and polyaniline (PANI). According to Dunn's calculation, the results demonstrate that the addition of 10 %wt PANI to both the positive (BM-P10) and negative (AC-P10) electrodes produced the maximum capacitance and energy density due to the enhancement of pseudocapacitive behavior. At a current density of 1 A g−1, the assembled BM-P10//AC-P10 ASC device at an operating voltage window of 1.5 V achieved a capacitance of 187 F g−1, an energy density of 58.4 W h kg−1, a power density of 1498.5 W kg−1, and a capacitance retention of 73.3 % after 10,000 cycles. This work will lead to the development of sustainable and effective supercapacitor for energy storage applications and also provide an alternative way for utilizing biomass materials in the energy sector.

head.ce:title: Exploring synergistic effects of polyaniline and carbon dots as capacitance enhancers on hybrid asymmetric brochantite/malachite//corn leaf-derived activated carbon supercapacitor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) have emerged as promising, high-performance energy storage devices owing to their broad operating voltage range, which significantly improves their energy and power densities. This work thus developed a hybrid ASC consisting of the copper hydroxy compound, brochantite/malachite (BM), as a positive electrode, and the corn leaf-derived activated carbon (AC) as a negative electrode, utilizing dual-hybrid capacitive mechanisms. BM is a promising positive electrode material due to its favorable faradaic reaction, whereas the corn leaf-derived AC displayed a high surface area of 898.3 m2 g−1 with micropores, enabling for a non-faradic storage mechanism at a negative electrode. The effects of different capacitance enhancers were further studied by investigating the capacitance efficiency of each electrode after adding corn leaf-derived carbon dot (CD) and polyaniline (PANI). According to Dunn's calculation, the results demonstrate that the addition of 10 %wt PANI to both the positive (BM-P10) and negative (AC-P10) electrodes produced the maximum capacitance and energy density due to the enhancement of pseudocapacitive behavior. At a current density of 1 A g−1, the assembled BM-P10//AC-P10 ASC device at an operating voltage window of 1.5 V achieved a capacitance of 187 F g−1, an energy density of 58.4 W h kg−1, a power density of 1498.5 W kg−1, and a capacitance retention of 73.3 % after 10,000 cycles. This work will lead to the development of sustainable and effective supercapacitor for energy storage applications and also provide an alternative way for utilizing biomass materials in the energy sector.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitor is one of the most attractive energy storage solutions because of its notable advantages, including high power output, fast charging and discharging, and extended lifespan [1,2]. The storage mechanisms of the supercapacitors are mainly categorized into two different processes. Electric doubled-layer capacitor (EDLC) involves the adsorption/desorption of ions through high surface-area carbon materials via electrostatic interaction (non-faradaic reaction) [2,3], leading to an excellent cyclic stability. Nevertheless, the energy density is limited, which hinders the development of long-lasting energy storage systems. The other type is a pseudocapacitor, which stores the charges via a rapid and reversible faradaic reaction. However, the cyclic stability is still a challenging problem because the redox reaction can cause material degradation, resulting in a dramatic reduction of capacitance over several charge-discharge cycles [4]. As compared to the symmetric supercapacitors (SSCs) using similar types of materials in both positive and negative electrodes, the combination of benefits from different types of electrode materials by fabricating asymmetric supercapacitors (ASCs) is one of the most productive processes to enhance the capacitive efficiency for the future energy storage devices [2].

By combining two distinct electrode materials, one with a non-faradaic capacitive mechanism and the other with a pseudocapacitive behavior via a faradaic reaction, ASC achieves extended voltage window and enhanced energy storage performance [5–7]. This hybrid configuration effectively balances high energy density with excellent power density of the device [8–11]. Based on their own research and domestic and foreign development situations, the selection of positive and negative electrode materials should be considered. Regarding the materials for positive and negative electrodes, the positive electrode is typically composed of materials with high pseudocapacitive property, such as transition metal oxides or conducting polymers, providing high energy density through redox reactions [12–14]. Transition metal oxides, for example, Mn3O4, WO3, NiCo2O4, and CuO, stand out for energy storage applications because of their excellent reversible redox property [15–21], enhancing electron transfer efficiency, ultimately improving capacitive performance [22]. Copper hydroxy compounds, including brochantite (Cu4SO4(OH)6) and malachite (Cu2CO3(OH)2), are also fascinating materials for use as a positive electrode due to their redox-active behavior, chemical stability, and economical cost [23,24]. Yuan et al. reported the application of (Ni,Cu) (OH)2CO3 electrode, achieving a high capacitance of 1017.3 F g−1 and a capacitance retention of 68.5 % after just 4000 cycles [25]. To improve capacitance retention, utilizing copper hydroxide-based materials as the positive electrode in ASC can enhance the device's cyclic stability and create new opportunities for advancing energy storage technologies.

In contrast, the negative electrode is typically composed of carbon-based materials, ensuring high power density and enabling rapid charging and discharging cycles through non-faradaic processes. Graphene, carbon nanotube, carbon nanofiber, and porous carbon, are widely used as electrode materials because of their high conductivity, high chemical stability, and non-toxicity [14,26]. Activated carbon (AC) is gaining interest for high-performance energy storage devices due to its extensive surface area, large pore volume, and well-defined pore size distribution, facilitating the adsorption of a large number of ions on its surface [27,28]. Additionally, a variety of precursors can be used to produce AC through cost-effective methods, such as natural precursors, biomass, and wastes [29–32]. Corn is one of the most significant economic crops in Thailand; however, its production generates substantial waste, leading to pollutant emissions and environmental issues [33]. Corn stover, which includes stalks, leaves, and husks, is rich in natural carbon-based compounds such as cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. These components make them an ideal raw material for AC production [34]. Our previous report demonstrated the AC preparation from corn leaf using the hydrothermal treatment, followed by the KOH activation at 800 °C. The corn leaf-derived AC, featuring a high surface area and porosity, was then combined with the corn starch electrolyte in the SSC device, achieving an energy density of 16.8 W h kg−1 [35]. The corn leaf is thus a viable candidate for producing AC as a negative electrode in ASC assembly.

To maximize the capacitive performance of the ASCs, the performance of each electrode improved by a capacitance enhancer must be investigated and optimized. Polyaniline (PANI) is a conducting polymer that has gained significant attention due to its unique electrical, optical, and mechanical properties, and controllable conductivity [36,37]. PANI has been reported as an additive to enhance the capacitive performance of various electrode materials, including copper- and carbon-based materials. Supchocksoonthorn et al. reported that the copper hydroxy electrode in the SSC device demonstrated remarkable capacitive performance, attributed to the presence of PANI, which induced pseudocapacitive behavior [38,39]. Li et al. described that the PANI could enhance the performance of AC negative electrode by forming rich surface redox-active sites [40]. Therefore, the incorporation between parent electrode materials and PANI would be an effective approach to strengthen the electrical conductivity and capacitive performance via enhancing the electron transfer between the electrode and electrolyte in supercapacitor [41]. Likewise, other types of capacitance enhancers are not limited to pseudocapacitive behavior. Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-based materials with zero-dimension. CDs have outstanding properties, such as high photoluminescence and good water solubility [42]. CDs have thus been studied and reported for use in energy storage devices because they can enhance the ion accessibility and facilitate the ion transfer during the charge/discharge processes [43,44].

The novelty and significance of this work lie in the fabrication of ASC using copper hydroxy compounds as the positive electrode and corn leaf-derived AC as the negative electrode. The comparison of different capacitance enhancers, including PANI and CDs, on both electrode performances was firstly investigated. The maximum performance of each electrode was then selected to fabricate the ASC (Fig. 1 ). Electrochemical performance and surface/diffusion contribution were analyzed to understand the synergistic effects of parent electrode materials with PANI and CDs. The results show that the addition of 10 %wt of PANI on both electrodes has been found to enhance the capacitance significantly. In comparison to our previous studies, the copper hydroxy compounds with the presence of PANI in the SSC device exhibited a remarkable performance in terms of capacitance and cyclic stability, but still had a narrow operating voltage window and a low energy density [39]. The ASC assembly in this work would, therefore, provide an alternative to enhance the device performance. Additionally, employing biomass to produce the negative electrode offers a cost-efficient strategy for minimizing the manufacturing expenses of energy storage devices. This study indicates that our fabricated ASC has promising potential for practical applications. The insights obtained will provide a basis for future advancements in high-performance, durable, and economical supercapacitors.

Corn leaves were supplied by a nearby corn farm in Phatthana Nikhom, Lop Buri, Thailand. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) was purchased from Acros. Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4⋅5H2O), 85 % ortho-phosphoric acid (H3PO4), 37 % hydrochloric acid (HCl), potassium hydroxide (KOH) pellets, and sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3) were bought from Carlo Erba. PANI in emeraldine salt form (organic sulfonic acid, MW > 15,000), and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Solvents and chemicals used in the experiments were not further purified. All solutions were prepared with deionized (DI) water. Conductive carbon paper with a resistance of less than 3 mΩ was used as a current collector to prepare electrodes. A cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO 1 kDa) from Spectrum Labs was used to purify the CD solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AC and CD from corn leaf
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for preparing the AC from corn leaf was reported in our previous work [35]. The cleaned and dried corn leaf (10 g) was first hydrothermally treated in a 1 M H3PO4 solution (100 mL) at 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the hydrothermally treated mixture was extracted with DI water to obtain the water-soluble, fluorescent CD solution. The CD solution was then neutralized and purified using a dialysis membrane to remove unreacted chemicals. The purified CD solution was finally freeze-dried to obtain the CD powder for further experiments.

The water-insoluble part after hydrothermal treatment, called hydrochar, was then used to prepare the AC. The dried hydrochar was mixed with KOH pellets at a weight ratio of 1:3 (hydrochar: KOH) in DI water (50 mL). The mixture was heated to 100 °C under vigorous stirring until all the water had vaporized. The KOH-impregnated hydrochar was then activated at 800 °C for 1 h in a tube furnace under an N2 atmosphere (99.95 % purity). After that, the AC was treated with 1 M HCl solution, and then washed with hot DI water to neutralize and remove any unreacted chemicals and contaminants. Finally, the AC was dried in a hot oven (80 °C) overnight before further experiments.

BM compound was prepared using the method from our previous work [39]. CuSO4⋅5H2O (3 g) and NaHCO3 (1.68 g) were separately dissolved in DI water (30 mL) at 100 °C. After mixing the two solutions, the greenish-blue solution and CO2 bubbles were produced. The BM crystal powder was then collected, rinsed with DI water, and dried at 50 °C overnight before being used.

A field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), Hitachi SU8230, was used to examine the surface morphology and elemental components of the materials. A specific surface area and pore characteristics were examined using a surface area and pore size analyzer (Microtrac, BELSORP MAX) via Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and non-local density functional theory with Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (NLDFT/GCMC) methods. An X-ray diffractometer (XRD; Rigaku, SmartLab), a Raman spectrometer (Macro-RAM, Horiba), a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet™ iS50, Thermo Scientific), and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos analytical, Manchester, UK) with monochromatic Al Kα1,2 radiation, were used to investigate the crystalline structure and functional groups of the materials. The morphology of CD in composite materials (AC-C10 and BM-C10) were observed using a JEM-2100 Plus, JEOL high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM). Contact angle measurement was carried out by dropping 3 M KOH electrolyte solution (1
μ
L) on the as-prepared electrode, and the angle was measured using a tensiometer (100Theta Lite, Biolin Scientific).

The AC electrodes were prepared by homogeneously mixing AC with carbon black (CB), and PVDF in an 80:10:10 ratio in NMP solvent. The mixed slurry was then coated onto conductive carbon papers (0.5
×
3 cm2) with fixed loading masses ranging from 1 to 2 mg cm−2. The as-prepared electrodes were dried in a hot air oven at 50 °C for 24 h before being used in electrochemical analysis. The AC composite electrodes were also fabricated using the same procedure by adding different capacitance enhancers, 10 %wt of CD or PANI powders, as shown in Table S1. Similarly, the pristine BM and BM composite electrodes were prepared using the same conditions as the AC electrodes. After examining the electrochemical effects of the capacitance enhancers, the amounts of the desired additive were adjusted to 5, 10, and 15 %wt in order to maximize the capacitive performance of both AC- and BM-composite electrodes. A 3 M KOH aqueous electrolyte solution, and a potentiostat/galvanostat (PGSTAT302N, Autolab, Metrohm) were used for all electrochemical analyses.

A three-electrode system consists of a platinum (Pt) wire counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a working electrode prepared on a carbon paper collector. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was studied by varying scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s−1. Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) was also investigated at different current densities from 1 to 4 A g−1.

In order to prepare a two-electrode configuration for CV, GCD, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements, two working electrodes with a diameter of 1 cm were assembled in a Swagelok cell. After the three-electrode measurement, the BM-P10 and AC-P10 were selected as the positive and negative electrodes, respectively, for ASC assembly, as they exhibited the highest capacitance. A cellulose filter paper soaked in 3 M KOH was used as a separator. SSC devices of AC-P10 and BM-P10, and ASC device of BM-P10//AC-P10, labeled as AC-P10_SSC, BM-P10_SSC, and BM-P10//AC-P10_ASC, respectively, were fabricated to compare the electrochemical performance. The EIS measurements of the Swagelok cells were carried out to study the resistance of the devices using a frequency range of 105 to 10−2 Hz, and an amplitude of 5 mV. The capacitance retention of the Swagelok cells were also investigated by running 10,000 charge-discharge cycles at 1 A g−1. The specific capacitance (Cs) was calculated from the discharge curves at every 200 cycles. The capacitance retention was calculated against the first cycle, which was set to be 100 %.

The ASC pouch cell was also prepared to demonstrate the practicality of the device by applying it to a small red LED device. The carbon papers (2
×
5 cm2) were prepared as current collectors for BM-P10 and AC-P10 electrodes with loading masses of 1–2 mg cm−2 per electrode. After preparing BM-P10 and AC-P10 electrodes, both electrodes were assembled together with the cellulose filter paper soaked in 3 M KOH as a separator, and then packed in a small plastic bag.

In the three-electrode system, the Cs was evaluated to determine the electrode performance for both positive and negative electrodes and to study the effects of the capacitance enhancers. The Cs (F g−1) was determined from the GCD analysis using Equation (1):
(1)

m Δ V
where I is an applied current (A) and m is the mass of the active material (g), Δt is the discharge time (s), and ΔV is a potential window (V).

In the two-electrode system including SSC and ASC devices, the energy density (E, W h kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) were analyzed using Equations (2) and (3), respectively [45]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and physical characteristics of AC, BM, and its composites
ce:section.ce:para:
SEM images (Figs. 2 and 3
) represent the size and morphology of the AC, BM, and their composite materials. The AC exhibited a rough surface with considerable porosity as a result of the KOH activation process (Fig. 2a). The SEM-EDS analysis reveals that the amounts of carbon and oxygen were 73.5 % and 26.5 % by weight, respectively, indicating that the synthesized AC has carbon as its main component. The sample known as AC-C10 was prepared by combining 10 %wt of CD in AC material. The CD particles were too small to observe the composite clearly, and they were unable to distinguish them from the AC using the SEM technique (Fig. 2b). However, the results of the SEM-EDS examination reveal that the weight percentages of oxygen, carbon, phosphorus, and sulfur were 53.8 %, 45.5 %, 0.5 %, and 0.2 %, respectively (Table S2). The increase in oxygen, phosphorus, and sulfur contents may be attributed to functional groups on the surface of the added CD. When PANI was mixed with the AC at a ratio of 10 % by weight, the sample was designated as AC-P10. The SEM images show the morphology of PANI, appearing as sheets mixed with the AC particles (Fig. 2c). The SEM-EDS analysis reveals that the amounts of carbon, oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen were 69.9 %, 16.8 %, 9.0 %, and 4.3 % by weight, respectively. An increase in N content was likely due to the addition of PANI, which contains nitrogen in its structure (Table S2). The presence of sulfur content in the AC-P10 material would come from the PANI structure in the form of emeraldine salt containing sulfonate groups.

The SEM images of brochantite/malachite, labeled as BM, reveal sheet-like and rod-like crystal structures of copper hydroxy compounds, forming flower-like aggregates (Fig. 3a). This morphology is consistent with previous studies that synthesized similar compounds [39]. The BM crystals were approximately 1–2 μm in length and 0.5 μm in width. The SEM-EDS mapping images show the elemental distribution within the compound, including copper (45.4 %), oxygen (37.8 %), carbon (13.3 %), and sulfur (3.6 %), as shown in Table S2.

When the CDs were added to the BM compound at 10 % by weight, labeled as BM-C10, the SEM images (Fig. 3b) show that the distribution of CD particles within the compound was not visible due to the very small size and the relatively low amount of CD added. Nevertheless, the elemental analysis confirmed an increase in carbon content from 13.3 % to 23.5 % of BM-C10 sample, along with the appearance of phosphorus and nitrogen at 0.5 % and 0.3 % by weight, respectively. These additional elements would originate from the functional groups presented on the CD surface. The SEM-EDS analysis of BM-P10 (Fig. 3c) could not detect nitrogen, possibly due to poor mixing between the BM compound and PANI. However, the carbon content increased from 13.3 % to 17.7 %, which could be attributed to the carbon components in the added PANI.

To further investigate the presence of CD nanoparticles in AC-C10 and BM-C10 samples, the HR-TEM analysis was then performed (Fig. S1). The HR-TEM images of AC-C10 (Figs. S1a and b) show the presence of nanoparticles with a size of about 5–8 nm, composing large carbon agglomerations, which illustrates the presence of CD in the composites. A CD lattice spacing of 0.2177 nm was observed (inset of Fig. S1b), indicating (100) graphitic plane structure [46].

The surface and pore characteristics of the materials were examined using the BET and NLDFT/GCMC methods. The BET technique provides the specific surface area of materials based on the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm, whereas the NLDFT/GCMC method provides pore size and pore volume data for microporous structure. The results show that the N2-adsorption desorption isotherms of AC and its composites (Fig. 4 a) correspond to type I isotherms, indicating monolayer adsorption or Langmuir monolayer adsorption [47]. The NLDFT/GCMC pore size distribution graphs of AC and its composites (Fig. 4b) show a pore size below 2 nm, highlighting the dominance of micropores in the structure. The BET specific surface area (SBET), pore size, and pore volume data are presented in Table S3. The SBET of corn leaf-derived AC was found to be 898.4 m2 g−1, which is relatively high for carbonaceous materials. A lower SBET of AC-C10 and AC-P10 is due to the addition of additives that can fill the AC micropore. Table S4 compares the materials characteristics of AC prepared from different biomass resources. Among various activating methods, KOH is one of most proper chemical activating agents for producing the biomass-derived AC because it was widely reported to generate microporous structure on carbon materials [48]. The SBET of AC prepared from different biomass sources shows relatively high in the range of 567–1169 m2 g-1. Interestingly, our corn leaf-derived AC demonstrated a remarkable pore volume, predominantly composed of micropores, surpassing other ACs. This characteristic makes it a promising candidate for use as a negative electrode in an ASC device [49].

The N2-adsorption desorption isotherm of BM and its composites (Fig. 4c) indicate type IV isotherms, confirming the presence of mesoporous structure [50]. The pore size distribution graph of the BM and its composites (Fig. 4d) is consistent with the isotherm results that the pore sizes are mostly larger than 2 nm. Similar to AC, the addition of additives results in a decrease in pore volume and surface area, as shown in Table S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of AC, BM, and its composites
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of AC (Fig. 5 a) shows two broad peaks: one in the range of 23–25°, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic plane of graphite, and another in the range of 42–44°, corresponding to the (100) plane. These results indicate that the AC synthesized from corn leaves contains conductive graphite structures, highlighting its potential as an electrode material in supercapacitors. The XRD spectrum of the synthesized BM compound (blue line) was compared to reference spectra of brochantite (Cu2(SO4) (OH)6) from JCPSD card No. 43–1458 (red line) and malachite (Cu2(CO3) (OH)2) from JCPSD card No. 01-0775-1163 (black line) (Fig. 5b). The spectrum of the synthesized compound shows peaks that represent both brochantite and malachite, with no peaks indicating impurities. The two copper hydroxy compounds were presented in relative proportions of 57 % brochantite and 43 % malachite. This analysis concludes that the BM copper hydroxy compounds synthesized via a one-step solvothermal method are successfully a brochantite/malachite mixture.

The elemental composition analysis of the brochantite/malachite compounds using XPS reveals that the survey spectrum (Fig. 5c) displays Cu2p, O1s, C1s, and S2p peaks at the binding energies of 935 eV, 532 eV, 285 eV, and 169 eV, respectively. The atomic concentrations of Cu2p, O1s, C1s, and S2p were 15.71 %, 48.53 %, 30.04 %, and 4.39 %, respectively, in accordance with our previous research [38]. Further examination of the high-resolution peaks for each element provided insights into the bonding characteristics (Fig. 5d–f). The high-resolution spectrum of Cu2p displays the bonding states of Cu2p1/2 and Cu2p3/2 at binding energies of 954.3 eV and 934.4 eV, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s reveals the Cu-O-H bond at 531.2 eV and water-adsorbed copper bonds at 532.2 eV. The high-resolution spectrum of S2p exhibits the Cu-S-O bonding at 168.5 eV, while a peak at 170 eV was attributed to sulfate groups (SO4 2−) presented in the compound structure.

The FT-IR and Raman spectra of all AC and BM composite materials were characterized as shown in Figs. S2 and S3. The FT-IR spectra of AC and its composites (Fig. S2a) present relatively similar characteristics of AC materials with the presence of broad peak of O-H stretching at about 3600–3000 cm−1, C=O stretching at 1064 cm−1. However, a more substantial vibration peak of C=O/C=C stretching at about 1525 cm−1 was observed after adding CDs or PANI in AC-C10 or AC-P10 materials, indicating the presence of CDs and PANI. The FT-IR spectra of BM materials (Fig. S2b) show the C-C bending mode at around 1385 cm−1, which would come from the malachite structure [39,51]. BM and its composite materials exhibited the vibrations of the Cu-OH bending, S-O stretching, and O-S-O stretching vibrations at 1045, 868, and 596 cm−1, confirming the sulfate structure of brochantite in pristine BM and its composites [39]. The appearance of a more substantial broad peak in the range of 3700 to 3200 cm−1 in BM-C10 material would be attributed to the addition of CDs containing hydrophilic oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectrum of BM-P10 exhibits a small peak at approximately 1300 cm−1, corresponding to the presence of a C-N stretching vibration, indicating the addition of PANI.

The Raman spectra of AC and its composite materials are presented in Fig. S3a. A Raman spectrum of pristine AC material exhibits a D-band at 1330 cm−1, indicating disordered carbon (sp3-carbon), and a G-band at 1609 cm−1, corresponding to graphitic carbon (sp2-carbon) [52]. The Raman spectrum of the AC-C10 material also shows the AC characteristics of D- and G-bands at 1315 and 1593 cm−1, respectively. In addition, the presence of 1489 cm−1 would be the sp3 carbon in sp2 graphitic carbon structure [53]. The Raman shifts at around 1245–1160 cm−1, presented in AC-C10 material, would be attributed to aliphatic hydrocarbons in CD due to incomplete carbonization. The Raman spectrum of the AC-P10 material exhibits the presence of C-N stretching of amine at 1170 cm−1, confirming the presence of PANI. The degree of graphitization (ID/IG) of AC, AC-C10, and AC-P10 are 1.57, 0.71, and 1.27, respectively. This demonstrates that the graphitic carbon content increased after adding CD or PANI to AC materials, which could enhance the electrical conductivity of the AC electrode material. Compared to previous reports, the Raman spectra of BM materials and their composites (Fig. S3b) exhibit characteristic peaks of copper hydroxy compounds, including brochantite and malachite [54–56]. The Raman shift at around 286 cm−1 is assigned to Cu-O bonding. The SO4 2− presented in the brochantite structure was observed at a Raman shift of about 630 cm−1. In addition, the existence of CO3 2− was observed at 1150-1220 cm−1, indicating the C=O stretching vibration from malachite. The higher intensity at 423–623 cm−1 in BM-C10 can be attributed to the increased intensity of the O-H stretching vibration resulting from the addition of CDs. The Raman shift at 1380 cm−1 can be attributed to the C-N stretching mode coupled with C-C and C-H vibrations in PANI or CD in AC-P10 or AC-C10 materials. Furthermore, an increase in the intensity of a Raman shift at 890 cm−1 in BM-C10 would also be ascribed as an increase in C=O stretching vibrations, corresponding to the oxygen-containing groups in CDs. These FT-IR and Raman analyses reveal that the addition of CD or PANI to the electrode material affects the electrically conductive structure of their parent materials, thereby improving the capacitive performance of the electrode materials in supercapacitor applications.

In order to maximize the ASC performance, the effect of capacitance enhancers to both positive and negative electrodes should be considered before ASC assembly. AC and its composite (AC-C10 and AC-P10) electrodes were electrochemically measured in three-electrode configurations with a 3 M KOH electrolyte solution. The CV curves at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (Fig. 6 a) for all three types of AC electrodes exhibit near-rectangular shape, with a potential window ranging from −0.45 to 0 V. This indicates that the main charge storage mechanism is through EDLC storage behavior, with charge stored in the negative potential range, making it suitable for use as a negative electrode in ASC.

GCD analysis at a current density of 1 A g−1 was conducted to investigate the charge and discharge capabilities of the as-prepared electrodes. The GCD curves (Fig. 6b) show a nearly symmetrical triangular shape, indicating efficient charge and discharge processes. However, it is noted that the AC-P10 electrode took a longer discharging time than other electrodes. A capacitance of the AC-P10 electrode was calculated to be 438.9 F g−1, whereas the AC electrode provided a capacitance of 288.9 F g−1. This confirms that the AC-P10 electrode has the highest capacitance due to the pseudocapacitive behavior of PANI, which is consistent with the largest area of the CV curve. The calculated Cs of the as-prepared electrodes is shown in Table S5.

An investigation on the optimal PANI dosage for enhancing capacitive performance was carried out. The ACs were further mixed with 5 % and 15 % by weight of PANI to prepare AC-P5 and AC-P15 electrodes, respectively. The results show that the AC-P10 electrode, containing 10 % PANI by weight, still provided the highest Cs, as evidenced by the largest area under the CV curve and the longest discharging time from GCD measurements (Fig. 6c and d). However, the excessive amounts of PANI in the AC-P15 electrode may block the pores on the AC surface, potentially hindering the efficiency of the EDLC mechanism.

The electrodes mainly containing copper hydroxy hybrid compounds, including BM, BM-C10, and BM-P10, were also studied for their electrochemical performance in a 3 M KOH electrolyte solution. The CV measurements were studied in a potential window from −0.7 to 0.2 V, exhibiting a quasi-rectangular shape (Fig. 7 a). There are also slight peaks at both positive and negative potentials, suggesting the involvement of a faradaic reaction in the charge storage mechanism. The GCD graphs (Fig. 7b) further confirm the involvement of faradaic reactions, as the charge-discharge cycles present non-linear shapes. As shown in Table S5, it was found that the BM-P10 electrode had the longest discharging time, with Cs of 485.6 F g−1. These suggest that the BM-P10 exhibited the highest charge storage efficiency due to enhanced pseudocapacitance mechanism from PANI.

Similar to AC electrodes, additional electrochemical studies were done to determine the optimal PANI concentration for increasing the charge storage efficiency. The results indicate that the BM-P10 also provided the highest Cs, as evidenced by the largest area under the CV curve and the longest discharging time in the GCD graph (Fig. 7c and d). Again, the excessive amounts of PANI may interfere with the faradaic reaction regions on the copper hydroxy compound surface, reducing the charge storage efficiency. Table S5 reveals that the BM-C10 electrode exhibited the lowest Cs compared to others. The reason that BM-C10 gave the lowest capacitance when compared to BM and BM-P10 is due to the presence of small-sized CDs and the incompatibility between CDs and BM, which are carbon-based materials and inorganic materials. These might block the charge transfer path between BM materials, leading to lower capacitance. On the other hand, PANI which is a long polymer chain can provide the charge transfer path by itself, leading to a higher capacitance.

To assemble the ASCs, the carbon electrode often serves as the capacitive-type negative electrode, which stores charges effectively at negative voltages and provides mainly the power source and moderate energy density of the ASCs. The metal-based positive electrode, which stores charges effectively at positive voltages, utilizes pseudocapacitive behavior produced by a reversible faradaic reaction between the electrolyte and electroactive species on the electrode surfaces [57]. The electrochemical performance of the two-electrode system was evaluated using a Swagelok cell. The voltage window measured in the two-electrode system of SSC with AC-P10 and BM-P10 electrodes are shown in the CV curves (Fig. 8 a). This indicates that the electrode primarily composed of the AC can operate in the negative voltage range (−0.8 to 0 V), while the electrode composed mainly of the BM compounds can operate in the positive voltage range (0–0.7 V). These imply that the ASC can be developed by combining these two electrode types, extending the overall measurable voltage window (Fig. 8b).

The study of the viable operating voltage range for the ASC (Fig. 8c and d) from 0 to 1.7 V reveals that the CV curve area and the GCD discharging time increase as the voltage window is extended, indicating higher capacitance and energy density. This is due to the fact that a wider voltage window facilitates more faradaic reactions and redox interactions between the electrode materials and the electrolyte, which improves the efficiency of charge storage mechanism. The reduction peaks at around 0.5 V are observed in the CV curves of ASC device, corresponding to the redox reaction of pernigraniline/emeraldine [58,59]. Compared to these previous studies, the slight peak shift is due to the use of different pH of electrolyte and also the effects of different composite materials. However, if the voltage range is extremely high, electrode material degradation and electrolyte breakdown can occur [60]. Based on the CV and GCD curves (Fig. 8c and d), the voltage window can be extended up to 1.5 V. When the voltage window increased to 1.7 V, the water-splitting reaction and polarization were observed after 1.5 V [61–63]. Therefore, the stable operating voltage range for charge storage is between 0 and 1.5 V. This asymmetric cell design broadens the voltage window compared to the SSCs, as the AC electrode extends the negative potential range, while the copper hydroxide electrode extends the positive potential range. At a current density of 1 A g−1, the Cs of the ASC was 187 F g−1, with energy and power densities of 58.4 W h kg−1 and 1498.5 W kg−1, respectively, indicating a higher capacitive performance than that of SSC systems (Table 1 ).

The CV analysis (Fig. 9 a) was conducted at scan rates ranging from 5 to 100 mV s−1, and the GCD analysis (Fig. 9b) was performed at current densities between 1 and 4 A g−1 for the assembled ASC The Cs decreased when increasing the scan rates and current densities due to the limitations of ion diffusion at high potential scan rates, as the electrolyte ions can only move through the outer surface of the pores. This restricted movement reduces the accessibility of the ions to the deeper internal pores of the electrode material, leading to a decrease in Cs at higher scan rates [64]. In addition, increasing the current density in GCD analysis shortened the charging and discharging times, leading to a reduction in Cs. This is because higher current density results in higher concentrations of ions in the electrolyte. In such conditions, ion transfer becomes inefficient, as the ions cannot move freely to fully interact with the electrode surface [65]. The coulombic efficiency of the ASC device at 1 A g−1, calculated from
t d
t c
× 100
%
when td and tc are discharging and charging times, respectively, was around 94 %, indicating high energy efficiency due to the reversibility of the electrode reactions [66]. However, the shape of charge/discharge curves are non-linear and asymmetric due to the presence of a series resistance and faradaic pseudocapacitive behavior [67,68].

The resistances of the fabricated supercapacitor systems were analyzed using EIS. The electrical circuit model of the ASC is shown in an inset of Fig. 9c, consisting of a solution resistance (Rs), a charge transfer resistance (Rct), and a Warburg element (W) [17,32,69]. The constant phase element (CPE), indicating EDLC (CPEEDL) and pseudocapacitance (CPEP) behaviors, were also combined in the circuit, suggesting the hybrid capacitance behavior of the ASC device [70]. The Nyquist plots (Fig. 9c) at the high-frequency region represent the Rs of AC-P10_SSC, BM-P10_SSC, and BM-P10//AC-P10 _ASC devices at 2.348, 4.545, and 2.890 Ω, respectively. The semicircle diameter observed in the high to mid-frequency region represents the Rct [71]. The semicircle diameter of AC-P10_SSC, BM-P10_SSC, and BM-P10//AC-P10_ASC devices were 2.141, 6.625, and 2.465 Ω, respectively. A straight line at a low-frequency region represents the Warburg resistance, corresponding to ion diffusion through electrode. The BM-P10_SSC device was also found to have the highest Warburg resistance as shown in the lowest slope of the plot in lower frequency, indicating poor diffusion of ions through the pores [38,72]. The BM-P10_SSC device exhibited the highest overall resistance, which may be due to the hydrophobicity of BM reducing the ion/charge transfer efficiency between the electrode and electrolyte. The hydrophobicity/hydrophilic properties of the AC and BM electrodes were investigated using a contact angle measurement (Fig. S4). It was found that BM electrodes exhibited higher contact angles of 3 M KOH electrolyte solution droplets, indicating more substantial hydrophobic properties.

The performance of the supercapacitor was further studied by evaluating its capacitance retention after 10,000 charge-discharge cycles (Fig. 9d). It was found that the symmetric AC-P10 supercapacitor retained 91.7 % of its initial capacitance, indicating the best long-term stability compared to other systems. This is attributed to the primary charge storage mechanism being based on EDLC, which offers high stability over multiple cycles. The capacitance retention of the symmetric BM-P10 supercapacitor dramatically reduced to 18.6 %. This substantial reduction in performance is attributed to the copper-based compound electrode materials undergoing redox reactions as part of the charge storage mechanism. The related electrochemical reaction leads to considerable volume expansion and limited long-term cycling stability [73]. Over repeated charge-discharge cycles, these redox reactions lead to material degradation, which significantly lowers the efficiency and overall capacitance retention of the metal-based supercapacitor. The assembled BM-P10//AC-P10_ASC device demonstrated lower capacitance retention of 73.3 % compared to that of the AC-P10_SSC device. The reduced efficiency is likely due to the use of a wider voltage window, which may cause the electrode material degradation due to redox reactions as shown in SEM images of the electrodes after cycling (Fig. S5). After several cycles, the morphology of the electrode materials changed. The AC-P10 electrode became agglomerated, which should result in reduced porosity and fewer charge storage sites. The BM-P10 electrode adopted flower-like morphology after cycling, which could lead to disconnected areas for charge transport. It is important to highlight that the formation of flower-like morphology is similar to the lithium dendrite formation in Li-ion batteries [74]. As these redox reactions become more prominent, they can lead to the breakdown of the electrode materials, resulting in a decline in capacitance retention. However, the capacitance retention of ASC is relatively higher than that of symmetric BM-P10 supercapacitor because of the hybrid mechanism of EDLC and pseudocapacitive behaviors. The practical application of the assembled ASC is demonstrated by preparing as a pouch cell connected with a red LED device (Fig. S6). A red LED can maintain the light for 5 s by connecting one ASC pouch cell, expressing the practicality of the assembled ASC.

The CV analysis at different scan rates, along with calculations based on the Dunn's method, is intended to investigate the charge storage mechanism of the electrodes, using the relationship in the equation

, where k1 represents the contribution of surface-controlled process, and k2 represents the contribution of diffusion-controlled process. The CV curves at 50 mV s−1 of AC, AC-C10 and AC-P10 electrodes reveal that the AC-based electrodes mainly exhibited surface charge storage mechanism, involving EDLC behavior (Fig. 10 a–c). Compared to the AC electrodes, the distribution of diffusion-controlled process in the AC-P10 electrode increased from 7 % to 33 %. This significant increase is attributed to PANI conductive properties, which enhance the pseudocapacitive performance in the supercapacitor. In the case of CD additive, the surface-controlled process distribution is slightly rose from 93 % to 94 %, which is correlated with the moderate increase of Cs in AC-C10 electrode compared to that of AC electrode (Table S5). The proportions of surface and diffusion charge storage mechanisms at different scan rates ranging from 10 to 100 mV s−1 were also observed (Fig. 10d). It was found that the proportion of surface charge storage increased when the scan rate increased. This suggests that higher scan rates promote surface mechanisms, likely because there is less time for ions to diffuse into the deeper pores on the electrode material surface [75]. The distributions of the surface and diffusion controlled-processes in BM, BM-C10 and BM-P10 electrodes were also determined. Similar to the AC-P10, the dramatic increase in diffusion contribution of BM-P10 from 38 % to 85 % led to the highest capacitive enhancement due to the presence of PANI as a pseudocapacitance enhancer (Fig. S7).

Fig. S8a shows a plot between log (current) and log (scan rate) from the CV analysis for determining whether the charge storage mechanism is surface-controlled, diffusion-controlled, or a combination of both based on the equation

. Here, b represents the slope of the graph. A value of b = 1 indicates a surface-controlled process, b = 0.5 suggests a diffusion-controlled process, and a b value between 0.5 and 1 indicates a combination of both surface-controlled and diffusion-controlled processes. When comparing the b-values of the AC, AC-C10 and AC-P10 electrodes, the b-values are 0.9721, 0.988 and 0.8585, respectively. All AC-based electrodes exhibit b-values close to 1, implying that the electron transfer mechanism was primarily governed by surface-controlled processes. However, the AC-P10 electrode, with the lowest b-value of 0.8585, demonstrates a higher contribution of diffusion-controlled processes, compared to other electrodes. This can be attributed to the addition of PANI, which enhances the pseudocapacitive diffusion-related charge storage [76]. [78]. The coefficient of determination (R2) is used to assess which mechanism dominates. The results show that the R2 value is closest to 1 in the current vs. scan rate plot of AC (R2 = 0.9979) and AC-C10 (R2 = 0.9974) electrodes, indicating that the charge storage mechanism is primarily surface-controlled. While the R2 value of AC-P10 electrode is closest to 1 (R2 = 0.9794) in the current vs. (scan rate)1/2 plot, supporting that the charge storage mechanism has a high contribution in diffusion-controlled electron transfer mechanism. This analysis confirms that the incorporation of PANI in AC-P10 enhances the diffusion-controlled process, contributing to an increase in pseudocapacitance behavior.

Based on the capacitance analysis and electrochemical measurements, it can be concluded that PANI incorporated with parent positive and negative electrode materials demonstrates the most efficient synergistic effect to enhance capacitive performance, primarily through a diffusion-controlled process, resulting from the pseudocapacitive behavior of PANI. In the positive electrode, the combination of BM and PANI could synergistically enhance the capacitance due to efficient charge transfer via electrostatic or hydrogen-bonding interactions between copper-based compounds and PANI [38]. For the negative electrode, the electrochemical performance of AC, mainly EDLC behavior, was also significantly improved by adding the pseudocapacitive PANI. The addition of PANI on AC porous structure could induce fast transportation of ions and electrons through a faradaic redox reaction [40]. Comparing to the surface-controlled enhancer like CD, CD can also enhance the capacitance of the AC parent negative electrode, but not as high as PANI. The CDs enhanced the electrode's surface wettability, leading to improved ion adsorption and interfacial interactions, which in turn boosted surface processes [79]. However, the CD surface-controlled enhancer would not be suitable for the copper-based electrode materials due to the incompatibility between hydrophilic and hydrophobic materials, as presented in the reduction of Cs after adding the CD into BM electrode. As a combination of EDLC and pseudocapacitive behaviors, the ASC assembly in this work consists of two distinct electrodes that can provide different operating voltages, thereby enhancing the voltage window and energy density of the device. Furthermore, it implies that a pseudocapacitance enhancer, such as PANI, would be a good candidate for maximizing the electrochemical performance in both the negative and positive electrodes of the ASC.

Table 2 compares the electrochemical performance of ASC using AC prepared from different biomass precursors as the negative electrode. Various types of materials have been used as positive electrodes in the ASC, including poly (2,3-dihydrothieno-1,4-dioxin) (PEDOT), nickel hydroxide-doped multi-walled carbon nanotubes (Ni(OH)2-MWCNTs), and other metal oxide compounds. Our work was the first to prepare the ASC using corn leaf-derived AC and copper-based compounds, demonstrating relatively high capacitive performance in terms of energy and power densities. Moreover, due to the simplicity of material preparation, our work demonstrates the potential of corn leaf as a raw material for the fabrication of high-energy-density supercapacitors.

This work developed a hybrid ASC comprising the combination of BM, copper hydroxy-based compounds, and corn leaf-derived AC as positive and negative electrodes. In addition to maximizing the electrochemical performance of the ASC, the effects of different capacitance enhancers, including PANI and CD, on the capacitive performance of each electrode were also investigated. It was found that adding 10 %wt of PANI, a pseudocapacitance enhancer, to both BM and AC electrodes was the most effective capacitance enhancer, which improved the specific capacitance by 152–176 % due to the enhancement of pseudocapacitive behavior, confirmed by an increase in the contribution of the diffusion-controlled storage mechanism. The ASC exhibited a broader operating voltage window of 1.5 V, compared to that of the SSC device, and had a high capacitance of 187 F g−1, an energy density of 58.4 W h kg−1, and a power density of 1498.5 W kg−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. Additionally, it maintained a capacitance retention of up to 73.3 % after 10,000 charge-discharge cycles. This work serves as a valuable model for developing the electrode performance in ASC made from copper-based compounds and AC in the presence of different capacitance enhancer mechanisms. It holds potential for future applications in energy storage devices for renewable energy systems and maximizes the use of agricultural waste.
